units.unit_name: Curricular Goals and Competencies - Matter, Physical World, Living World
topics: CG 1 – Explores the world of matter, its interactions, and properties at the atomic level

C 1.1 – Describes classification of elements in the Periodic Table, and explains how compounds (including carbon compounds) are formed based on the atomic structure (Bohr's model) and properties (valency).

C 1.2 – Investigates the nature and properties of chemical substances (distillation, crystallisation, chromatography, centrifugation, types and properties of mixtures, solutions, colloids, and suspensions)
C 1.3 – Describes and represents chemical interactions and changes using symbols and chemical equations (acid and base, metal and non-metal, reversible and irreversible)

units.unit_name: Curricular Goals and Competencies - Physical World Principles
topics: CG 2 – Explores the physical world around them, and understands scientific principles and laws based on observations and analysis

C 2.1 – Applies Newton's laws to explain the effect of forces (change in state of motion — displacement and direction, velocity and acceleration, uniform circular motion, acceleration due to gravity) and analyses graphical and mathematical representations of motion in one dimension
C 2.2 – Explains the relationship between mass and weight using universal law of gravitation, and connect it to the laws of motion

C 2.3 – Manipulates the position of object and properties of lenses (focus, centre of curvature) to observe image characteristics and correspondence with a ray diagram, and extends this understanding to a combination of lenses (telescope, microscope)

C 2.4 – Manipulates and analyses different characteristics of the circuit (current, voltage, resistance) and mathematises their relationship (Ohm's law), and applies it to everyday usage (electricity bill, short circuit, safety measures)
C 2.5 – Defines work in scientific terms, and represents the relationship between potential and kinetic energy (conservation of energy) in mathematical expressions
C 2.6 – Demonstrates the principle of mechanical advantage by constructing simple machines (system of levers and pulleys)
C 2.7 – Describes the origin and properties of sound (wavelength, frequency, amplitude) and differences in what we hear as it propagates through different instruments

C 2.8 – Explores interconnected systems and phenomena that support and affect life on Earth (hydrosphere, biosphere, atmosphere, geosphere, cryosphere and their interrelationships, earth processes, hazards, etc.)
key_concepts: Earth systems (hydrosphere, biosphere, atmosphere, geosphere, cryosphere)

sourceSessionPlan.title: Life processes

teacherLessonNotes.sessionOverview: This session introduces the fundamental concept of 'Life Processes' for Class X. Students will explore why living organisms need to perform specific functions (nutrition, respiration, transport, excretion) to survive and maintain homeostasis. The focus is on defining life processes through observation and real-world analogies before diving into biological mechanisms.
prerequisiteKnowledge: Understanding that all living things need food
Awareness of breathing as a daily activity
learningOutcomes: Define life processes and explain their necessity for survival.
Distinguish between autotrophic and heterotrophic nutrition with examples.
Identify the four main life processes in humans: Nutrition, Respiration, Transport, Excretion.
Explain why complex organisms require specialized systems (like blood vessels) unlike simple ones.

teachingPlan.topic: Nutrition Types & Overview of Other Processes

lessonPurpose: To establish that 'living' is defined by the continuous performance of specific functions.
To differentiate between simple organisms (single-celled) and complex ones regarding how they obtain food.
teachingSequence: Start with a hook: Ask students if a car needs fuel to move. Connect this to humans needing food.
Define 'Life Processes' as the basic functions that keep an organism alive (nutrition, respiration, transport, excretion).
Discuss why we need these processes: Growth, repair, energy for movement/thinking.
Introduce Nutrition types: Autotrophic (self-made) vs Heterotrophic (others made). Use plants and animals as examples.
Briefly list the other three processes without deep diving yet to set the stage for future sessions.
Conclude with a comparison of simple vs. complex organisms regarding these needs.
guidedPractice: Ask students to identify which life process is happening when they run (Respiration + Transport).
Have students categorize given examples: Potato plant, Mushroom, Human being.
Quick check: Why do we need a transport system? (Answer: To move food/oxygen from one part of the body to another).

lessonBlocks.title: Introduction
teacherPrompt: If I put petrol in a car but never drive it, does it stay new? What happens if we don't eat for days?
What is the one thing that separates a rock from a dog?
explanation: Living things are not static; they are dynamic. They constantly change and need energy to maintain their structure.
Non-living things do not perform internal functions like eating or breathing on their own.
examples: A car needs fuel (external input) just as a human does, but the car doesn't 'digest' it; humans convert food into energy internally.
boardWork: Non-living: Do not perform life processes
checkUnderstanding: Can you name one thing a plant does that a stone doesn't?
expectedAnswers: Plants make food (photosynthesis), stones do not.

lessonBlocks.title: Concept of Life Processes
teacherPrompt: Why can't a single-celled organism like Amoeba just sit still and wait for food to come to it?
What happens if the heart stops beating?
explanation: Simple organisms (like Amoeba) have their whole body in contact with water, so they don't need complex transport.
Complex organisms (Humans/Plants) are large; cells deep inside cannot reach food/oxygen directly. They need a system to move things around.
The four main processes: Nutrition (eating), Respiration (breathing/gas exchange), Transport (moving materials), Excretion (waste removal).
examples: Amoeba vs Human: Amoeba eats by engulfing; Humans eat, digest in stomach, and blood carries nutrients to cells.
boardSteps: Draw a simple flow: Food -> Digestion -> Blood -> Cells.
checkUnderstanding: Which process removes waste from our body?

lessonBlocks.title: Nutrition Types & Overview
teacherPrompt: How do plants get their food if they don't have mouths?
What is the difference between a cow and a mushroom in terms of eating?
explanation: Autotrophic Nutrition: Organisms make their own food (e.g., Green Plants using sunlight).
Heterotrophic Nutrition: Organisms depend on others for food (e.g., Animals, Fungi/Mushrooms).
We will study these in detail later; today we just need to know the difference.
Briefly mention that Respiration is breaking down this food to release energy.
checkUnderstanding: Is a mushroom an autotroph or heterotroph?
expectedAnswers: Heterotroph (it absorbs food from dead matter).

conceptFlow.definition: The basic functions performed by living organisms to maintain life and ensure survival.
conceptFlow.coreExplanation: Living things are not just physical objects; they are dynamic systems. To stay alive, grow, repair damage, and reproduce, they must continuously perform specific tasks known as Life Processes. These include taking in food (Nutrition), breaking it down for energy (Respiration), moving materials within the body (Transport), and removing waste (Excretion).
conceptFlow.importance: Without these processes, an organism dies. They are the definition of 'life'.
conceptFlow.whyStudyIt: To understand how our bodies work and why health is important.
conceptFlow.relationshipWithPrevious: Builds on the basic definition of living vs non-living from lower grades.
conceptFlow.relationshipWithFuture: Leads to detailed study of Photosynthesis, Human Digestive System, Circulatory System, and Excretory System in subsequent sessions.
teacherMoves: Use the 'Car' analogy to explain energy input.
examples: A car needs fuel; a human needs food. Both need maintenance (repair).
visuals: Diagram of an Amoeba vs Human showing contact with environment.

sourceSessionPlan.introduction: Imagine a car. It needs petrol to run, oil for maintenance, and it produces exhaust fumes. A human body is similar! We need food (petrol), we breathe (maintenance/energy release), and we remove waste (exhaust). These continuous activities are called Life Processes. Without them, the 'engine' of life stops.

theory.overview: Life processes are the basic functions performed by living organisms to maintain life and ensure survival. They include nutrition, respiration, transport, and excretion. Organisms obtain food either by making it themselves (autotrophs) or by consuming others (heterotrophs).
keyPoints: Living things are dynamic; they must constantly perform functions to survive.
There are four main life processes: Nutrition, Respiration, Transport, Excretion.
Autotrophic nutrition involves making food from simple substances using sunlight (e.g., plants).
Heterotrophic nutrition involves depending on other organisms for food (e.g., animals).
theory.detailedContent:
Life processes are the fundamental activities that keep an organism alive. Unlike non-living things, living organisms grow, repair themselves, and respond to their environment through these continuous functions.

1. **Nutrition**: The process of taking in food and using it for energy, growth, and repair. It is divided into:
   - *Autotrophic*: Organisms like green plants make their own food from carbon dioxide and water using sunlight (photosynthesis).
   - *Heterotrophic*: Organisms like animals and fungi cannot make their own food and must consume other organisms.

2. **Respiration**: The process of breaking down glucose to release energy for cellular activities. This is different from breathing, which is just the physical exchange of gases.

3. **Transport**: In complex organisms (like humans), nutrients and oxygen need to be moved from one part of the body to another via blood vessels because cells are not in direct contact with the environment.

4. **Excretion**: The removal of waste products produced by metabolic activities, such as urea or carbon dioxide.

activities.name: The Breathing Demonstration & Pulse Check
instructions: Ask students to place a hand on their chest and breathe deeply for one minute.
Instruct them to count the number of breaths taken in that minute (Respiration Rate).
Have students find their pulse at the wrist or neck immediately after exercise (e.g., 10 jumping jacks) and compare it with resting rate.
Discuss: 'Why did your breathing speed up? What is happening inside to support this?'
Connect the physical sensation of breath to the invisible process of gas exchange.

activities.name: Nutrition Analogy Workshop
instructions: Divide students into groups. Assign each group a type of nutrition: Autotrophic (Plants) or Heterotrophic (Animals).
Task: Create a flowchart on chart paper showing the 'ingredients' and 'source' for their assigned mode.
Autotrophs must list Sunlight, CO2, Water, Chlorophyll. Heterotrophs must list Food from others.
Groups present their charts explaining how they obtain energy differently but achieve the same goal (survival).
Teacher synthesizes: 'Both are essential life processes; one makes food, the other consumes it.'

activities.name: Circulatory System Simulation
instructions: Select two students to be the Heart (Pump) and Lungs. Others form a circle representing blood vessels.
The 'Heart' pushes an imaginary ball of oxygenated blood through the circle to the 'Lungs'.
'Lungs' exchange CO2 for O2, then pass it back to the 'Heart' which distributes it to the rest of the class (body cells).
Discuss: What happens if the heart stops? Why do we need a transport system?
Reinforce that this is an active process requiring energy and coordination.

items.answer: C. Photosynthesis (as a standalone non-living activity)
items.explanation: Photosynthesis is the process by which autotrophs make food, but 'photosynthesis' itself as an isolated chemical reaction isn't listed as one of the four broad life processes in this context; Nutrition, Respiration, Transport, and Excretion are the standard list.
Autotrophs are organisms that synthesize their own food from inorganic substances.
Autotrophic nutrition requires sunlight, chlorophyll (pigment), carbon dioxide, and water.

items.answer: B. They depend on plants or other animals for food.
items.explanation: Heterotrophs cannot make their own food and must consume organic matter from others.

items.answer: Definition: Life processes are the continuous biological activities essential for maintaining life. Reasoning: Without these, an organism cannot survive or grow (e.g., no energy without respiration). List: 1. Nutrition, 2. Respiration, 3. Transport, 4. Excretion.
